title: Code-Based 10 Mark Questions Bank with R Code
subject: Probability Distributions and Statistical Analysis in R

questions.question: Write an R program to compute the two-tailed p-value for a Student's t-distribution with t = -2.43 and df = 13. Also compute the upper 1% critical value of the F-distribution with df1 = 2 and df2 = 7. Display the results clearly and explain the role of pt() and qf().
questions.code:
t_p_value <- 2 * pt(-2.43, df = 13)
f_critical <- qf(0.01, df1 = 2, df2 = 7, lower.tail = FALSE)
cat('Two-tailed p-value:', t_p_value, '\n')
cat('Upper 1% critical value of F(2,7):', f_critical, '\n')

questions.question: Write an R program to generate a sequence from -3 to 3 and use dnorm(), pnorm(), qnorm(), and rnorm() on it. Print the density values, cumulative probabilities, 95th percentile, and 10 random samples. Comment on the purpose of each function.
questions.code:
x <- seq(-3, 3, length.out = 100)
density_values <- dnorm(x)
cdf_values <- pnorm(x)
percentile_95 <- qnorm(0.95)
random_samples <- rnorm(10)
print(density_values)
print(cdf_values)
cat('95th percentile:', percentile_95, '\n')
print(random_samples)

questions.question: Using the built-in faithful dataset in R, write a program to display the summary() and fivenum() of the eruptions variable. Interpret the output in terms of minimum, quartiles, median, mean, and maximum.
questions.code:
data(faithful)
summary(faithful$eruptions)
fivenum(faithful$eruptions)

questions.question: Write an R program to generate a stem-and-leaf plot for faithful$eruptions. Explain how the plot preserves the original data values and how it helps in understanding the distribution.
questions.code:
data(faithful)
stem(faithful$eruptions)

questions.question: Write an R program to plot a histogram of faithful$eruptions with probability scaling, add a kernel density estimate using density(), and add a rug plot. Explain the significance of prob = TRUE, density(), and rug().
questions.code:
data(faithful)
hist(
  faithful$eruptions,
  breaks = seq(1.6, 5.2, 0.2),
  prob = TRUE,
  main = 'Histogram of eruptions'
)
lines(density(faithful$eruptions, bw = 0.1))
rug(faithful$eruptions)

questions.question: Write an R program to plot the empirical cumulative distribution function of faithful$eruptions using ecdf(). Explain how the empirical CDF estimates P(X <= x) directly from data.
questions.code:
data(faithful)
plot(ecdf(faithful$eruptions), do.points = FALSE, verticals = TRUE)

questions.question: Subset faithful$eruptions to values greater than 3, plot the ECDF of the subset, and overlay a fitted normal CDF using pnorm() with sample mean and variance. Write the complete R code and explain how the comparison is useful.
questions.code:
data(faithful)
long <- faithful$eruptions[faithful$eruptions > 3]
plot(ecdf(long), do.points = FALSE, verticals = TRUE)
x <- seq(3, 5.4, 0.01)
lines(
  x,
  pnorm(x, mean = mean(long), sd = sqrt(var(long))),
  lty = 3
)

questions.question: Write an R program to draw a Q-Q plot for the long eruption times from the faithful dataset and add the reference line using qqline(). Explain how deviation from the straight line indicates non-normality.
questions.code:
data(faithful)
long <- faithful$eruptions[faithful$eruptions > 3]
par(pty = 's')
qqnorm(long)
qqline(long)

questions.question: Write an R program to perform the Shapiro-Wilk test and one-sample Kolmogorov-Smirnov test for the long eruption times from the faithful dataset. Display the test statistics and p-values, and interpret the results.
questions.code:
data(faithful)
long <- faithful$eruptions[faithful$eruptions > 3]
shapiro.test(long)
ks.test(
  long,
  'pnorm',
  mean = mean(long),
  sd = sqrt(var(long))
)

questions.question: For the two independent samples A and B of latent heat measurements, write an R program to create comparative boxplots. Explain how medians, spread, and possible outliers can be visually compared.

questions.topic: welch t test and variance test
questions.question: Write an R program to perform Welch’s two-sample t-test and F-test for equality of variances on the samples A and B. Report the null hypotheses, test statistics, p-values, and conclusions.
Write an R program to perform the classical two-sample t-test on samples A and B by assuming equal variances. Compare its result with Welch’s t-test.
Write an R program to perform the Wilcoxon rank-sum test on samples A and B. Explain why this test is called non-parametric and how it differs from the t-test.

questions.topic: split and for loop with regression lines
questions.question: Write an R program that generates random vectors x and y, splits them according to a random factor ind, and then plots each group separately with a fitted regression line using lsfit(). Explain the use of split() and for loop in this context.
questions.code:
set.seed(1)
x <- rnorm(50)
y <- rnorm(50)
ind <- factor(sample(1:3, 50, replace = TRUE))
xc <- split(x, ind)
yc <- split(y, ind)
for (i in seq_along(xc)) {
  plot(xc[[i]], yc[[i]])
  abline(lsfit(xc[[i]], yc[[i]]))
}

questions.topic: custom two sample t statistic function
questions.question: Write a user-defined R function twosam(y1, y2) to manually compute the pooled-variance two-sample t-statistic. Apply it to the samples A and B and verify the result.
Write an R function bslash(X, y) that uses QR decomposition to compute regression coefficients. Create a small design matrix and response vector, apply the function, and explain the output.
questions.code:
bslash <- function(X, y) {
  X <- qr(X)
  qr.coef(X, y)
}
Xmat <- cbind(1, rnorm(10))
yvar <- rnorm(10)
bslash(Xmat, yvar)

questions.question: Using the nycflights13 dataset, write an R program to compute the mean, median, and standard deviation of arrival delay and departure delay. Display the results neatly and interpret them.
questions.code:
library(nycflights13)
flights <- nycflights13::flights

mean_arr <- mean(flights$arr_delay, na.rm = TRUE)
median_arr <- median(flights$arr_delay, na.rm = TRUE)
sd_arr <- sd(flights$arr_delay, na.rm = TRUE)

cat('Arrival Delay:\n')
cat('Mean:', round(mean_arr, 2), '\n')
cat('Median:', median_arr, '\n')
cat('SD:', round(sd_arr, 2), '\n\n')

cat('Departure Delay:\n')
cat('Mean:', round(mean(flights$dep_delay, na.rm = TRUE), 2), '\n')
cat('Median:', median(flights$dep_delay, na.rm = TRUE), '\n')
cat('SD:', round(sd(flights$dep_delay, na.rm = TRUE), 2), '\n')

questions.question: Write an R program using dplyr to group flights by carrier, compute the standard deviation of arrival delay for each carrier, sort them, and identify the most consistent airline.
questions.code:
library(nycflights13)
library(dplyr)
flights <- nycflights13::flights

carrier_sd <- flights %>%
  group_by(carrier) %>%
  summarise(sd_arr_delay = sd(arr_delay, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = 'drop') %>%
  arrange(sd_arr_delay)

print(carrier_sd)
cat('Most Consistent Airline:\n')
print(carrier_sd[1, ])

questions.question: Write an R program to calculate flight speed in miles per hour using distance and air_time from the nycflights13 dataset. Compute the mean, median, and skewness of speed and plot its histogram using ggplot2.
questions.code:
library(nycflights13)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(moments)
flights <- nycflights13::flights

flights_speed <- flights %>%
  filter(!is.na(air_time), air_time > 0) %>%
  mutate(speed_mph = distance / (air_time / 60))

cat('Mean Speed:', round(mean(flights_speed$speed_mph, na.rm = TRUE), 2), '\n')
cat('Median Speed:', round(median(flights_speed$speed_mph, na.rm = TRUE), 2), '\n')
cat('Skewness:', round(moments::skewness(flights_speed$speed_mph, na.rm = TRUE), 3), '\n')

ggplot(flights_speed, aes(x = speed_mph)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, fill = 'skyblue', color = 'black') +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = 'Distribution of Flight Speeds', x = 'Speed (mph)', y = 'Frequency')

questions.question: Write an R program to group departure delay into intervals using cut() with breaks from -100 to 500 in steps of 50. Display the frequency table and compare the grouped data with the original mean and standard deviation.
questions.code:
library(nycflights13)
library(dplyr)
flights <- nycflights13::flights

flights_grouped <- flights %>%
  filter(!is.na(dep_delay)) %>%
  mutate(delay_group = cut(dep_delay,
                           breaks = seq(-100, 500, by = 50),
                           include.lowest = TRUE))

print(table(flights_grouped$delay_group))
cat('Original Mean:', round(mean(flights$dep_delay, na.rm = TRUE), 2), '\n')
cat('Original SD:', round(sd(flights$dep_delay, na.rm = TRUE), 2), '\n')

questions.question: Write an R program to compute the 25th, 50th, and 75th percentiles of departure delay in the nycflights13 dataset. Explain what these quartiles indicate about the data.
questions.code:
library(nycflights13)
flights <- nycflights13::flights
quantile(flights$dep_delay, probs = c(0.25, 0.5, 0.75), na.rm = TRUE)

questions.topic: random variable distribution of departure delay
questions.question: Treat departure delay as a random variable X. Write an R program to compute E[X], Var(X), skewness, and kurtosis. Plot the histogram with density curve and comment on the distribution shape.
questions.code:
library(nycflights13)
library(ggplot2)
library(moments)
flights <- nycflights13::flights

X <- flights$dep_delay[!is.na(flights$dep_delay)]
cat('E[X]:', round(mean(X), 2), '\n')
cat('Var(X):', round(var(X), 2), '\n')
cat('Skewness:', round(moments::skewness(X, na.rm = TRUE), 3), '\n')
cat('Kurtosis:', round(moments::kurtosis(X, na.rm = TRUE), 3), '\n')

ggplot(data.frame(X), aes(x = X)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 60, fill = 'lightblue', color = 'black') +
  geom_density(linewidth = 1, color = 'red') +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = 'PDF of Departure Delay', x = 'Departure Delay', y = 'Density')

questions.question: Write an R program using ggplot2 to visualize the probability density of departure delay with a histogram and overlaid density curve. Label the axes and title appropriately.
questions.code:
library(nycflights13)
library(ggplot2)
flights <- nycflights13::flights

X <- flights$dep_delay[!is.na(flights$dep_delay)]

ggplot(data.frame(X), aes(x = X)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 60, fill = 'lightblue', color = 'black') +
  geom_density(linewidth = 1, color = 'red') +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = 'PDF of Departure Delay', x = 'Departure Delay', y = 'Density')

questions.topic: chi square test on categorical data
questions.question: Write an R program to create a contingency table between flight origin and whether the flight arrived on time, then perform Pearson’s chi-square test. Display the table, test result, and print whether the association is significant.
questions.code:
library(nycflights13)
library(dplyr)
flights <- nycflights13::flights

fl_test <- flights %>%
  filter(!is.na(arr_delay)) %>%
  mutate(arrived_on_time = abs(arr_delay) <= 5)

tab_origin <- table(fl_test$origin, fl_test$arrived_on_time)
print(tab_origin)

chi_result <- chisq.test(tab_origin)
print(chi_result)

if (chi_result$p.value < 0.05) {
  cat('Significant association between origin and on-time arrival.\n')
} else {
  cat('No significant association.\n')
}

questions.question: Write an R program that stores package names in a vector, checks whether each package is installed, installs missing packages, and then loads them using a for loop.
